item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm incredibly fond of this article and I like it a lot. That said, I think it would be much better served by swapping the order in which the two dreams are described. Setting up with the classically creepy one and then following with the subversion rings better than beginning with the subversion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm super into this. Short, weird, disturbing, the pineapple as a focal point is delightful. I really like SCPs that include a ton of unanswered questions and just act as sort of a crystallization point of weirdness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting. Im Curious as to what would happen if a subject (D-Class) were to take a heavy Ambien, or enter some sort of forced comatose-like- state) to pursue this nightmare long enough to find out what this Entity wants?

Could it be seeking some sort of Agenda? Or perhaps trying to warn us of something bigger?

I agree with others that this could be explored more. +1 from me for now, in hopes of this article growing into something deeper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do feel that Stonefish's shorter articles are their best. |+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like the beginning of something, rather than an entire whole. I see an interesting start, with the pineapple somehow being worse than the emaciated child for a reason we don't know, but it doesn't seem to end (or lead into something interesting) in something quite clear enough.

Something should be added to the end, or happen in the middle to suggest a certain kind of end. Novote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a bit mixed about this one until I read this comment which switched it to an upvote to me. The idea of the pineapple being the real threat is brilliant, but I wish there'd be a bit more evidence of that in the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this is missing something. The effect is nice, the ending works, both variations are pretty cool. The containment procedures aren't half-bad. All of the individual elements here get their jobs done but the article is less than the sum of its parts. Maybe a little more development to the Variations or something like that might be enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article strikes me as something made from the human subconscious trying to rationalize something it can't and has lost or isolated inside itself. I'm not sure why it is rationalizing it as a pineapple but that is hardly an issue. The human brain makes some weird connections when it doesn't have an exact definition or concrete concept to attach something it is perceiving to.

Easy +1, the idea of some hidden aspect of the human mind brought out by the anomalous exposure the antimemetics team goes through is some prime real estate that this article builds a fascinating house upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is gonna be a no-vote for me. The tension in the document drops as you go and the exclusion of drugs in the anxiety treatment seems extremely unnecessary, as the only difference that occurs is the content of the dream and the pills that appear afterward. The nightmare disorder and the involuntary screaming upon waking are identical.
That said, it's not a downvote. With some work (maybe an addendum to really sell the danger of anomalous nightmare disorder in antimemetics researchers and a definitive number of researchers suffering from this, I'm not sure what would work), this could be a really cool skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quickly want to point out that the classically creepy second variation, with the deformed child and approaching monster, is what you see on amnestics, which make you forget what you saw.

Whatever the pineapple is, it’s worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not 100% sold on second variation (if only because it falls short of the high bar set by Terror Pineapple) but I love how total detachment from intelligible causality is treated as an occupational hazard here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is okay. I needed to get it out of my head but it's not that scary I don't think. It's fine, sorry.
